coredata.dc:title: DFT Studies and investigation on catalytic activity of Cu-complex immobilized on modified carbon dots for carbon-carbon homocoupling reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Ghahremani, Maryam
Farzaneh, Faezeh
Ghiasi, Mina
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) modified with branched polyethylene imine was prepared at 200ºC and designated as BPEI-CDs followed by complexation with CuI, CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2I, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl and Cu(en)2Cl2. After characterization of the prepared heterogeneous compounds by means of XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM and XPS, they were tested as catalysts for the oxidative homo-coupling of phenylacetylene, 4‑tert-butylphenylacetylene, 1-ethynyl-4-flourobenzene and 1- pent‑1-yn-3ol. It was found that Cu (I) I and Cu (I) bipyridene show conversion yields of 67–100% with excellent selectivities (95–100). The catalysts were also recovered and used for four runs without significant decrease in catalytic activities. Based on the density functional theory (DFT) studies on activity of substrates, 1-ethynyl-4-flourobenzene was found to show the most activity, consistent with those reported in experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cu(I) complexes
C-C homocoupling
Polyethylene imine
Citric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for terminal alkyne homocoupling reactions using BPEI-CDs-Cu (I, II) nanocomposites
Geometry optimization of substrates and HOMO-LUMO analysis
The mechanism of homocoupling reaction in the presence of [(NH2)2N(NH)Cu1+(I)] as catalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would appreciate Alzahra University for financial supporting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of Branched Polyethylenimine-functionalized Carbon dots (BPEI–CDs).
Of Catalyst BPEI–CDs-Cu complex compounds.
The FT-IR spectra of (a) Citric Acid, (b)Branched Polyethylenimine and (c)BPEI-CD, (d) Cu(BiPy)2I and (e) Cu(BiPy)2 complex@BPEI-CD .
XRD pattern for(a) BPEI-CD, (b) CuI, (c) CuI @BPEI-CD (d) Cu(BiPy)2I and Cu(BiPy)2 @ BPEI-CD.
(a) SEM (b) TEM images with EDXpattern and mapping of the BPEI-CD.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM images with (c) EDAX pattern and (e-g) elemental mapping of CuI@BPEI-CD-CuI.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM images and (c) EDAX pattern and (d-f) mapping obtained for the BPEI-CD- Cu(BiPy)2I.
XPS spectra of the CuI@ BPEI-CD: (a) survey, (b) Cu 2p.
XPS spectra of the BPEI-CD- Cu(BiPy)2I: (a) survey, (b) Cu 2p.

Effect of time on the percentage conversion and selectivity for the homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene catalized by 100 mg catalyst CuI@ BPEI-CDs in the presence of sodium carbonate as base and Benzonitrile as solvent.

Effect of the amount of catalyst on the percentage conversion and selectivity for the homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene catalyst CuI@ BPEI-CDs in the presence of sodium carbonate as base and benzonitrile as solvent.
Effect of solvent on homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene with catalyst CuI@ BPEI-CDs in the presence of sodium carbonate as base.
Effect of base on the percentage conversion and selectivity for the homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene catalized by 100 mg catalyst CuI@ BPEI-CDs in the presence of Benzonitrile as solvent.
. The effect of recyclability of the catalyst CuI@ BPEI-CDs for the homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene at optimum condition
The FTIR spectra of CuI@ BPEI-CDs before using and after using as catalyst
XRD patterns of CuI@ BPEI-CDs before using and after using as catalyst
Representation of the optimized geometries, HOMO and LUMO structures of different substrates including Sub1, Sub2, Sub3, Sub4 and Sub5 from top to down respectively.
The optimized geometry of model catalyst with some structural details

Calculated energy profile for the proposed mechanism for the substrate 4 through the reaction path with optimized geometry of substrate, intermediates, transition state and product, the reported values are according to Scheme1.

Calculated energy profile for the proposed mechanism for the substrate 5 through the reaction path with optimized geometry of substrate, intermediates, transition state and product, the reported values are according to Scheme 1.
Calculated energy profile for the proposed mechanism for different substrates trough the reaction path, the reported values are according to kcal/mol.
Proposed mechanism for the homocoupling reaction
Conversion and selectivity of phenylacetylene with different amounts of the CuI@BPEI-CD as catalyst at different times.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: phenylacetylene (1 mmol), Na2CO3 (2 mmol), benzonitrile (4 mL) heated at 100 ̊C under O2.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the homocoupling reactions of different substrates catalyzed by CuI@BPEI-CD.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction was carried out in the presence of diphenylamine as radical scavenger.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of catalytic reaction of phenylacetylene in the presence of various catalysts copper (I, II).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All the reactions were carried out with phenylacetylene (1 mmol), catalyst (0.1 g), Na2CO3 (2 mmol) Benzonitrile (4 ml) and heated to 100 ̊C under O2, Time(6 h).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated different energy through the reaction path for different substrates according to kcal/mol.
Comparison of figure of merit of the present work with other studies in the literature.

row.entry: Reactions were conducted with (Z)-arylvinyl bromides, DBU, DMF under MW (120 W) for 2 min, at r.t,8 h.
the presence of a chelating agent (the most efficient is TMEDA) and an amine as organic base, in the presence of dioxygen as oxidant,72 h.
1-butylamine, AcOEt, in air at room temperature,8h
CH3CN under ambient temperatureand the ratio of alkyne/CuI/NBS/DIPEA was 1:0.5:1:0.5,2h

alkyne, 0.1 equiv
of piperidine and 0.02 equiv of CuCl in toluene, at 60°C for 5 h
piperidine, at room temperature under air, 6 h.
Phenylacetylene,Na2CO3 Benzonitrile and heated to 100 ̊C under O2 ,6 h.
Phenylacetylen,Na2CO3, Benzonitrile and heated to 100 ̊C under O2 ,6 h.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cu/Cu2O nanoparticles on reduced graphene oxide.

head.ce:title: DFT Studies and investigation on catalytic activity of Cu-complex immobilized on modified carbon dots for carbon-carbon homocoupling reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) modified with branched polyethylene imine was prepared at 200ºC and designated as BPEI-CDs followed by complexation with CuI, CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2I, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl and Cu(en)2Cl2. After characterization of the prepared heterogeneous compounds by means of XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM and XPS, they were tested as catalysts for the oxidative homo-coupling of phenylacetylene, 4‑tert-butylphenylacetylene, 1-ethynyl-4-flourobenzene and 1- pent‑1-yn-3ol. It was found that Cu (I) I and Cu (I) bipyridene show conversion yields of 67–100% with excellent selectivities (95–100). The catalysts were also recovered and used for four runs without significant decrease in catalytic activities. Based on the density functional theory (DFT) studies on activity of substrates, 1-ethynyl-4-flourobenzene was found to show the most activity, consistent with those reported in experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the subclass of zero-dimentional carbon nanomaterials with the average size smaller than 10 nm [1]. Synthesis of the CDs has attracted attentions due to the unique chemical, electrochemical and use as catalyst, electrocatalyst [2], and applications such as electrochemical biosensing, optical biosensing, bioimaging and so on [3–10]. There are two general methods for preparation of CDs as up-down and bottom-up. Whereas in the former, CDs are prepared using carbon nanotubes with different chemical and physical methods [1,12,13], in the latter, they are prepared by means of citric acid [14], glycerol [15] or glucose [16,17], microwave, solvothermal and hydrothermal methods [11,18].Utilization of CDs as catalyst in the field of some oxidation and reduction processes such as Suzuki−Miyaura reactions, biodiesel production and ring opening reactions are rare [19]. Acetylenic compounds have many applications in the preparation of optoelectronic devices [20], organic materials [21], natural products and biomolecules synthesis [22,23]. The straight approach for the synthesis some 1,3-diynes is the coupling of two terminal alkynes, as expanded by Glayser and Hay [24–26]. Due to the high abundance, low cost and low toxicity of copper, it has been widely considered as excellent catalyst for the synthesis of 1, 3-dieynes [27]. Heterogenous catalysts including Cu(II)- modified hydroxylapetite (Cu-Hap) [28], Cu(BTC)2-metal organic frameworks [29], polymer supported copper complex and Cu/Cu2ONPs@rGo catalyst have been used for alkyne coupling [30], and Glaser homo-coupling reactions, respectively [31].

The novelty of this work was preparation of carbon dots and modification with branched poly-(ethylene amine) (BPEI) [32], as a good heterogeneous for immobilization of different ccomplexes in some organic processes such as terminanal alkyne homocoupling reactions.

All chemicals were purchased from Merck and Sigma-Alderich chemical companies and used without further purification. The FTIR spectra (4000–400 cm-1) were recoreded on Bruker instrument with KBr pellets. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) was obtained on Philips XL 30 SEM equipped with an X-ray energy dispersive detector. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were taken from Zeiss- EM10C-100KV.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 250 Xi Mg using X-ray resource were carried out. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was carried out with STOE-STADV using Cu Kα radiation. The products were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC) (Agilent Technology, 6890 series GC System, HP-5 Phenyl Methyl Siloxane Capillary, Carrier Gas He) and GCMS– using an 5973 Network Mass selective Detector, 6890 Network with GC system column; HP Phenyl Methyl Siloxane Capillary.
BPEI-CDs was prepared using the previously reported procedure [32].

A suspension of PEI-CQD containing PEI-CDs (1 g in 25 mL distilled water) was added to a solution containing the desired Cu (I, II) X compounds (CuI, CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2I, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl, Cu(en)2Cl2 (1 mmol in 25 mL water), and stirred at 80 ᵒC for 24 h. The obtained solid was washed for several times and dried in an oven at 60 ᵒC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for terminal alkyne homocoupling reactions using BPEI-CDs-Cu (I, II) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Terminal alkynes (1 mmol), catalyst (0.1 g) in solvent (3 mL) and sodium carbonate (0.211 g, 2 mmol) were added to reaction flasks and heated at 100 ᵒC within 6 h, under O2 atmosphre. The obtained products were analyzed by GC and GC-Mass.
BPEI-CD was synthesized using the previously reported by precipitations of citric acid (CA) and branched poly (ethylenimine) (BPEI) (Fig.1 ) [32].

CuI supported on BPEI-CD was prepared using the previously reported procedure as shown in Fig. 2
[26]. Cu(I,II)@BPEI-CDs-nanocomposites compounds such as CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2I, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl, Cu(en)2Cl2, Cu(acac)2I, Cu(acac)2Cl, Cu(acac)2Cl2) were prepared and immobilized as reported for CuI.

The FTIR spectra of CA, BPEI, BPEI-CD were identical to those of authentic samples [32] the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, C=O, CH2, COOH for CA appeared at 3293, 3293, 2921–2565, 1428–1359 and 1143 cm-1, respectively (Fig. 3 a) [33,34]. For BPEI, vibrations of NH (3417, 1591 cm-1), CH (2929, 2831, 1466 cm-1), -N (938 cm-1) were clearly observed (Fig. 3b). The vibration of CONH group of BPEI-CD displayed at 1704 cm −1. Cu(BiPy)2I complex displays three bands at 1590, 1437 and 760 cm-1 due to the C=C, C=N and CH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively [35]. Upon immobilization of Cu(BiPy)2I complex on the BPEI-CD, the peaks of Cu(BiPy)2I complex and BPEI-CD can both be seen (Fig. 3d-e).

XRD patterns of BPEI-CD, CuI, CuI@ BPEI-CD, Cu(BiPy)2I, Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD shown in Fig. 4 a–c are consistent with those reported [32,36]. The crystalline structure of CuI was confirmed by XRD analyses (Fig. 4b). The XRD results of CuI is consistent with JCPDS 82–2111 No. [37]. The obtained results indicate the presence of CuI on surface of the prepared nanoparticles. Moreover, the presence of Cu(BiPy)2I on BPEI-CD was confirmed by XRD results.

The SEM and TEM images, EDAX pattern and mapping of BPEI-CDs, CuI@ BPEI-CD and Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD given in Figs. 5 a–f, 6 a–f and 7 a–f respectively, indicate the formation of paticle size of 5-10 nm, and the presence of only C, N and O in BPEI-CDs. As seen in Fig. 7a–d, the particle size increased from 10 to 30 nm after immobilization of Cu(BiPy)2I on BPEI-CD.
The FTIR , XRD patterns and SEM images of other Cu compounds of CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl, Cu(en)2Cl2, are shown in supplementary (see Figures S1-S13).

The XPS spectrum of CuI@ BPEI-CD as a nanocatalyst shows the presence of C, N, O, Cu and I elements in CuI@ BPEI-CD (Fig. 8 a). For CuI, two peaks appeared at 930 and 950 eV due to Cu 2P3 and Cu 2P1, respectively (Fig. 8b). The obtained results consistent with those reported previously [38,39].

The XPS spectrum of Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD shows the presence of C, N, O, Cu elements in the Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD (Fig. 9 a). For Cu(BiPy)2I two peaks are observed at 930 and 950 eV in the XPS spectrum, corresponding to Cu 2P3 and Cu 2P1, respectively (Fig. 9b).

To optimize the reaction conditions, the effect of catalyst amount, time, base type and solvent, phenylacetylene was used as substrate model for alkyne–alkyne homocoupling reaction in the presence of CuI @BPEI-CD as catalyst. It was found that the highest conversion and selectivity toward the formation of the corresponding product was obtained when the reaction heated at 100ºC in benzonitrile within 6 h in the presence of 100 mg of CuI @BPEI-CD (Figs. 10 , 11 and Table 1 ).

When the reactions were carried using different solvents such as acetonitrile, dimethylforamide, ethanol and benzonitrile (Fig. 12 ), reaction proceeded to completion (100% conversion and selectivity) in benzonitrile.

A variety of bases such as sodium acetate, triethylamine (TEA) 1-pentyn-3-ol, 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2] octane (DABCO) and sodium carbonate were also tested for homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene using CuI@ BPEI-CD as catalyst. The reactions were proceeded with 80, 53, 100, 100% conversions respectively (Fig. 13 ).

Other derivatives of phenylacetylene such as 4-methyl phenylacetylene, 4‑methoxy phenylacetylene, 4-fluoro phenylacetylene, 4‑tert‑butyl phenylacetylene 4-n‑butyl phenylacetylene) were examined under the optimized reaction conditions. It was found that substituted phenylacetylenes either with electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups leads to moderate to excellent conversion yields (Table 2 ). Whereas the phenylacetylene bearing electron-withdrawing group F afforded the highest yield within 2 h (90%), other para-substituted phenylacetylenes containing electron-donating groups resulted in lower yields (14–87%) under similar reaction time (2 h) (Table 2).

The proposed mechanism shown in Scheme 1 is suggested [40,41]. Initially, the intermediate I is generated upon treatment of phenyacetylene with copper. The dimers II, III are subsequently formed from the interaction of copper site with the substrate triple bond. Then, reduction of Cu(I) to Cu(0) leads to homocoupling reaction and formation of the 1, 3-diyne product followed by oxidation of copper ions and regeneration of catalyst. Consistent with the suggested mechanism, the phenylacetylene bearing the para-electron-withdrawing group F is expected to afford the highest conversion yield due to the higher stability of the CCu bond generated in the intermediate I (Scheme 1). In contrast, since the para-substituted electron-donating phenylacetylenes destabilize the CCu bond in I either via direct conjugation or hyperconjugation, the formation of the 1, 3-diyne products in lower conversion yields is anticipated.

When homocoupling were carried out in the presence of diphenylamine as radical scavenger (Table 2) [40,41], no considerable product was formed. It can be concluded that the reaction has proceeded via a radical pathway. Based on the results indicated in Table 2, the effect of the counter anion as I › Br › Cl is also concluded. Moreover, copper (I) was shown to be more efficient than the copper (II).

The reusability of CuI@ BPEI-CD as catalyst was investigated in the homocoupling reaction of phenylacetylene under the optimized reaction conditions. At the end of reaction, the catalyst was separated from reaction mixture, washed and dried before using in the next runs. After using the recovered catalyst with fresh starting materials, it was concluded that the catalyst could be reused for four consecutive cycles with slight decrease in activity from 100% to the 92% (Fig. 14 ).

The powder XRD and the FT-IR spectra of the catalyst after using in 4 cycles showed that the overall structure of the material remained intact (Figs.15 and 16
). Therefore, the stability of the CuI@BPEI-CD catalyst during these reaction is concluded.

Based on the obtained results shown in Table 3 , it was found that either CuI@BPEI-CD or Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD and Cu(en)2I@ BPEI-CD showed the most and the least catalytic activity, respectively toward CC coupling. Moreovere, the higher activity of bipyridine as ligand than that of ethylene diamine was also concluded.
Computational studies were also carried out in order to compare of reactivity of different substrates toward the homocoupling reactions.
All technical details are reported in supplementary [42–45]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometry optimization of substrates and HOMO-LUMO analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The geometries of all studied substrates including phenylacetylene (Sub1), 4-methyl phenylacetylene (Sub2), 4‑methoxy phenylacetylene (Sub3), 4-fluoro phenylacetylene (Sub4) and 4 ‑tert‑butyl phenylacetylene (Sub5) have been optimized in the gas phase and then in the solvent at the B3LYP/6–311+G** level. The first column from left side in Fig. 17 shows the optimized structure of all substrate in solvent. In the following to predict the chemical reactivity of studied substrates the HOMO-LUMO analysis were performed. Fig. 17 depicts the DFT computed surface plots of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Furthermore, the HOMO and LUMO energy gap is so important to understand chemical molecular properties. The molecules having low energy gap show high reactivity and hence low thermodynamic stability as well. The values of the energy gap (∆Eg) for substrate 1 to 5 including phenylacetylene, 4-methyl phenylacetylene, 4‑methoxy phenylacetylene, 4-fluoro phenylacetylene and 4‑tert‑butyl phenylacetylene were found to be 0.428, 0.427, 0.429, 0.426 and 0.432 eV respectively. So the order of reactivity of studied substrates is predicted as 4-fluoro-phenylacetylene > 4-methyl-phenylacetylene > 4‑methoxy- phenylacetylene > phenylacetylene > 4‑tert‑butyl phenylacetylene is in good agreement with the experimental results.

According to the obtained results in experimental section, it was found that CuI@ BPEI-CD and Cu(BiPy)2I@ BPEI-CD show the most and Cu(en)2I@ BPEI-CD the least catalytic activity toward CC coupling. So to study catalytic mechanism for different substrates using of quantum mechanical calculations, the model of CuI@ BPEI-CD including [(NH2)2N(NH)Cu1+(I)] were constructed and optimized at the same level of calculations, B3LYP/6–311+G**. Fig. 18 presents the optimized geometry of model catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of homocoupling reaction in the presence of [(NH2)2N(NH)Cu1+(I)] as catalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction pathway for different substrates has been studied based on proposed mechanism in Scheme 1. Initially the geometry of intermediate I, dimer II, dimer III and product were fully optimized for different substrates. In following the frequency calculation were performed in order to study the probability of transition state for the all abovementioned structures. Interestingly, no imaginary frequencies were observed in the frequency calculation results except for dimer II which had one imaginary frequency for all studied substrates. To confirm that the dimer II is appear as a transition state in reaction path, the geometry of this dimer was re-optimized as transition state. The presence of one imaginary frequency in the result of frequency calculations confirm this hypothesis. The accuracy of transition state was double-check with QST3 and IRC calculations which confirm that the dimer II as transition state through the reaction path. The calculated energy profile for entire reaction for substrate 4 and 5 is given in Fig. 19 and 20
and for other substrate is presented in supporting information, Figs. S14—S16. As seen the energy barrier for substrate 4 and 5 is the less and the most value respectively which is in good agreement with experimental results. It is interesting to note that, the variation energy of total reaction, ∆Erxn for all studied substrates except substrate 5 with the highest energy barrier is exothermic. Finally, the energy profile to comparison for all substrates is presented in Fig. 21 . Also the variation of energy through the reaction path is collected in Table 4 . The analysis of calculated results show that the formation of Cu(I)-acetylide intermediate I which is generated from the reaction of phenylacetylene with copper site for all substrate is exothermic, ∆EintI. For example, the stabilization energy to form intermediate I for sub4 is about -12.01 kcal/mol which has the most negative value rather than other substrates. Moreover, it was found that substituted phenylacetylenes with electron-withdrawing groups such as F decrease the barrier energy which is to afford the highest conversion yield. Finally, intramolecular rearrangement with the aid of Cu(I) to Cu(0) reduction leads to homocoupling reaction and of the 1,3-diyne product which is exothermic reaction for all substrates (see also supplementary ). Moreover, comparison of energy variation to formation of intermediate I, transition state and intermediate II indicate that the tendency of different substrates to interact with model catalysis for homocoupling reaction is as following: Sub 5 < Sub 1 < Sub 3 < Sub 2 < Sub 4. The obtained results are consistent with experimental ones.

In conclusion, Sub 4 with lowest barrier energy of 6.81 kcal/mol, has been characterized as the best substrate with respect to others shown in Table 4. In contrast, Sub 5 with the most energy barrier of 31.78 kcal/mol, is predicted as the most unsuitable substrate for the homocoupling reaction.
Comparison of the catalytic activity of Cu-polyamine functionalized carbon dots nanocomposites on CC homocoupling with other reported systems indicated in Table 5 seems promising.

Some copper compounds such as CuI, CuBr, CuCl, CuBr2, CuCl2, Cu(bpy)2I, Cu(bpy)2Cl, Cu(bpy)2Cl2, Cu(en)2I, Cu(en)2Cl, Cu(en)2Cl2, were immobilized on the modified carbon dots and used as catalysts for CC homocoupling reactions. Based on the obtained results, it was found that CuI@ BPEI-CD and Cu(BiPy)2I@BPEI-CD show the most and Cu(en)2I@BPEI-CD the least catalytic activity toward CC coupling. On the other hand, the catalytic activity of Cu(I) is more active than Cu(II). Moreovere, bipyridine as ligand exhibis more activity than ethylene diamine. Different derivatives of phenylacetylene such as 4-methylphenylacetylene, 4‑methoxy phenylacetylene, 4-fluorophenylacetylene, 4‑tert-butylphenylacetylene and 4-n‑butyl phenylacetylene were examined for Glaser-homocoupling under optimized reaction conditions. The high yield and selectivity toward the formation of the corresponding products together with stability and reusability of catalyst for four consecutive runs without losing significant activity is promising. The DFT calculation results were found to be in agreement with those obtained experimentally.
Maryam Ghahremani: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Faezeh Farzaneh: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mina Ghiasi: Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authours declare that there are no conflicts of interest regarding the publication of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would appreciate Alzahra University for financial supporting.
Also the fast calculations center of Alzahra University is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.135862.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: preparation, properties, and application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Selin, N. Sahiner, Carbon dots: preparation, properties, and application, Nanocarbon Compos. (2019) 651–676, 10.1016/B978-0-08-102509-3.00022-5.
Z. Kang, Y. Liu, Catalytic applications of carbon dots, Carbon Nanoparticles Nanostructures (2016) 257–298, 10.1007/978-3-319-28782-9-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: classification, properties, synthesis, characterization, and application in health care- an updated review (2018-2021)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme-free electrochemical nano-immunosensor based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for cardiac biomarker determination

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of N-rich carbon quantum dots from porphyrins as efficient probes for bioimaging and biosensing in living cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Live cell bioimaging with carbon dots produced in situ by femtosecond laser from intracellular material

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of penicillin G residues in milk based on dual-emission carbon dots and molecularly imprinted polymers

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: large-scale synthesis, sensing and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, S.H. Yu, Carbon dots: large-scale synthesis, sensing and bioimaging, Mater. Today 19(2016) 382–393, 10.1016/j.mattod.2015.11.008.
